title: E2E test for project creation flow
description: Add an end-to-end test that covers the full project creation flow: (1) authenticated user submits the new project form with a valid repo name and description, (2) GitHub repo is created, (3) project directory is cloned to ~/projects/<repo_name>/, (4) beads is initialized in the directory, (5) bdui sidecar is started, (6) Ralphy opencode session is created, (7) project is stored in the DB, (8) user is redirected to /projects/:slug. The test must run against the real VPS environment (not mocked) to catch PATH and subprocess issues. Clean up created repos and directories after the test.
acceptance_criteria: The e2e test passes end-to-end. If any step fails, the test fails with a clear error message identifying which step broke. The test is run as part of the CI/test suite.
notes: Use a test GitHub repo name like 'justralph-e2e-test-<timestamp>' to avoid collisions. Clean up via GitHub API and rm -rf after the test completes.

title: Bug: 'bd' not found in PATH when web app spawns subprocesses
description: Creating a project fails with '[Errno 2] No such file or directory: bd'. The web app spawns subprocesses (e.g. to run 'bd init') but the subprocess PATH doesn't include ~/.local/bin/ where bd is installed (/home/nico/.local/bin/bd). Fix by either: (1) using the full path to bd in all subprocess calls, or (2) ensuring the web app's subprocess environment includes ~/.local/bin/ in PATH.
acceptance_criteria: Creating a project successfully runs bd commands without 'No such file or directory' errors.
notes: bd is at /home/nico/.local/bin/bd. The same issue likely affects other tools (opencode, etc.) — audit all subprocess calls and ensure they use full paths or a correct PATH.

title: Bug: login button redirects to 404 GitHub page
description: Clicking the 'Sign in with GitHub' button redirects to a GitHub page that returns 404. The OAuth authorization URL is likely malformed or pointing to the wrong endpoint. The correct GitHub OAuth authorization URL is: https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize?client_id=<GITHUB_CLIENT_ID>&scope=repo,read:user&redirect_uri=https://justralph.it/auth/callback
acceptance_criteria: Clicking 'Sign in with GitHub' redirects to a valid GitHub OAuth authorization page where the user can approve access.
notes: GITHUB_CLIENT_ID is in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.env. This may be related to the GitHub App → OAuth migration (just-ralph-it-cdf) — the auth URL may still be using GitHub App endpoints.

title: Replace GitHub App auth with GitHub OAuth
description: The current implementation uses a GitHub App for authentication and repo operations. Replace it with standard GitHub OAuth. The OAuth app needs scopes: 'repo' (create and push to repos) and 'read:user' (get username). Store the OAuth access token server-side associated with the session. Use it for all GitHub API calls (repo creation, pushes). Remove all GitHub App-specific code (private key, app ID, installation tokens). The OAuth callback URL is https://justralph.it/auth/callback. Credentials (GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET) are already in .env.
acceptance_criteria: User can sign in with GitHub OAuth. Repo creation and pushing works using the OAuth token. No GitHub App code remains. Re-signing in works without needing to uninstall anything.
notes: GITHUB_CLIENT_ID and GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET are in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.env. Required scopes: repo, read:user.

title: Bug: subagent hangs waiting for interactive input
description: A subagent prompted for user input and hung the entire Ralph loop. All subagents must run non-interactively — they must never wait for stdin or prompt the user. Ralph's prompt must explicitly instruct subagents to always use non-interactive flags (e.g. apt-get -y, cp -f, npm --yes, etc.) and never call any command that could block waiting for input. If a subagent encounters a situation requiring user input, it must fail fast and report back rather than hang.
acceptance_criteria: No subagent ever hangs waiting for input. All shell commands use non-interactive flags. If a command requires interaction, the subagent fails fast and reports the issue.
notes: Add to Ralph's prompt: 'All subagents must run non-interactively. Never use commands that prompt for input. Always use non-interactive flags: apt-get -y, cp -f, mv -f, rm -f, npm --yes, etc. If a command requires interaction, fail fast and report back.'

title: ralph.py: check VPS resources and stop at 90% usage
description: At the start of each loop iteration (before claiming the next issue), ralph.py checks RAM and disk usage. If either exceeds 90%, ralph.py stops the loop gracefully (same as the .stop flag behavior) and fires a browser push notification: 'Ralph stopped: VPS resources at [X]% [RAM/disk]. Free up space or upgrade before continuing.' Check RAM with psutil or /proc/meminfo, disk with shutil.disk_usage('/').
acceptance_criteria: When RAM or disk hits 90%+, ralph.py exits cleanly after the current issue and sends a push notification. Below 90%, the loop continues normally.

title: Bug: re-signing in with GitHub App doesn't redirect properly or save token
description: When a user signs in for a second time (e.g. after logging out or session expiry), they are not redirected properly to justralph.it and the installation token is not saved. The user has to uninstall the GitHub App and reinstall it to get a working session. Likely cause: the OAuth callback flow only handles new installations, not re-authorizations of existing ones. The callback must handle both cases: new installation and existing installation re-auth.
acceptance_criteria: A user can sign in multiple times without needing to uninstall the GitHub App. Each sign-in correctly redirects to /projects and saves a valid installation token.
notes: GitHub App callbacks receive different parameters for new installations vs re-authorizations. Handle both the 'installation_id' (new) and 'code' (re-auth) parameters in the callback.

title: Bug: 403 error when creating GitHub repo during project creation
description: Clicking 'Create project' returns '403 Client Error: Forbidden for url: https://api.github.com/user/repos'. The GitHub App has repo creation permissions but the API call is failing. Likely cause: using the wrong token type (user OAuth token vs GitHub App installation token), or the installation token is not being generated/used correctly for repo creation. GitHub Apps must use installation tokens, not user tokens, for repo operations.
acceptance_criteria: Creating a project successfully creates a GitHub repo without a 403 error.
notes: GitHub Apps use installation tokens (generated from the app's private key + installation ID) for API calls, not user OAuth tokens. Check that the repo creation call uses the correct installation token. See https://docs.github.com/en/apps/creating-github-apps/authenticating-with-a-github-app/generating-an-installation-access-token-for-a-github-app

title: Bug: SECRET_KEY falls back to insecure 'dev' default
description: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] falls back to the hardcoded string 'dev' when the SECRET_KEY environment variable is not set. This is a security vulnerability — session cookies can be forged. Ralph must generate a cryptographically secure random SECRET_KEY and add it to the .env file on the VPS. The app must also fail loudly (refuse to start) if SECRET_KEY is not set in production, rather than silently using the insecure default.
acceptance_criteria: SECRET_KEY is set to a secure random value in .env. The app refuses to start if SECRET_KEY is missing or equals 'dev' in production.
close_reason: Fixed: Added python-dotenv to load .env, added SECRET_KEY validation that raises RuntimeError if missing or 'dev' in production. Tests cover all cases. .env already has secure 256-bit key.

title: Bug: Ralph working on multiple issues simultaneously
description: Ralph is claiming and working on multiple issues at the same time (currently 'just-ralph-it-dsu' and 'just-ralph-it-0oj' are both in_progress). Ralph must only work on one issue at a time — claim it, complete it, close it, then pick up the next one. This is likely caused by ralph.py not checking for already in-progress issues before claiming a new one, or subagents claiming issues independently.
acceptance_criteria: At any given time, only one issue is in_progress. Ralph never claims a new issue while another is already claimed.
notes: Fix ralph.py to check 'bd list --status in_progress' before claiming a new issue. If one is already in progress, resume it instead of claiming a new one.
close_reason: Fixed: ralph.py now checks for in-progress issues before claiming new ones, uses atomic --claim with check=True, and resumes existing in-progress issues instead of claiming new ones

title: ralph.py: add logging.basicConfig so logger.info output reaches stdout
description: ralph.py uses logger.info() for all output (Results.ALL_DONE, Results.STOPPED, opencode lines, etc.) but never configures logging with basicConfig(). This means no output reaches stdout, which breaks _watch_ralph() reason detection in routes.py (it checks the last stdout line). Regression from just-ralph-it-1pc which converted print() to logger.info() without adding basicConfig().
close_reason: Fixed: stdout handler now uses %(message)s format so _watch_ralph() can match bare strings like 'NO MORE ISSUES LEFT' and 'Stopping as requested.' File handler retains full format with timestamps.

title: Use logging instead of print @ralph.py
Bug: ralph.log only updates after session finishes, not in real-time
description: ralph.py stdout is being buffered and only flushed to ralph.log when a session completes, instead of line-by-line in real-time. This means the Terminal tab in the UI shows no output while Ralph is working. Fix by either: (1) running ralph.py with PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1, or (2) adding sys.stdout.flush() after every print() call in ralph.py, or (3) using print(..., flush=True) throughout.
acceptance_criteria: ralph.log updates line-by-line in real-time as Ralph works. The Terminal tab in the UI shows output as it happens, not only after a session ends.

title: Bug: claimed issues not showing as in-progress
description: Ralph reports claiming issues via 'bd update <id> --claim' but the issues remain in 'open' status instead of transitioning to 'in-progress'. This makes it impossible to track what Ralph is actively working on and breaks the beads workflow.
acceptance_criteria: When Ralph claims an issue, it immediately shows as 'in-progress' in bd list and the beads UI.
notes: Check if 'bd update <id> --claim' is being called correctly and if the Dolt auto-commit policy is causing writes to not persist. Also verify Ralph is running bd commands in the correct project directory.

title: Bug: production not updated after code changes
description: Ralph is making code changes but they are not being reflected at justralph.it. After every code change, Ralph must restart the production systemd service so the new code is served. This should be part of Ralph's standard workflow when closing any issue that involves code changes.
acceptance_criteria: After Ralph closes a code-related issue, the changes are live at justralph.it without manual intervention.
notes: Check the systemd service name with 'systemctl list-units --type=service'. Restart it after deploying changes: 'systemctl restart <service>'.

title: Bug: /projects route returns 404 after GitHub auth redirect
description: After signing in with GitHub, the user is redirected to /projects but gets a 404 Not Found. The route exists in the spec but is either not registered, not handling the redirect correctly, or the auth callback is redirecting to the wrong URL. This is a critical flow — it's the first thing a user sees after login.
acceptance_criteria: After signing in with GitHub, the user lands on /projects and sees their projects list (or an empty state with a 'New Project' button). No 404.
notes: Fix the root cause AND ensure there are tests covering the full auth → redirect → /projects flow so this cannot regress.

title: Update Ralph's prompt: system prompt changes don't need TDD
description: Ralph's TDD clarification must be extended: changes to system prompts, opencode config, and other non-code text files also do not need automated tests. However, Ralph must still manually test that the behavior works as expected after making those changes (e.g. send a test message to Ralphy to verify the prompt change has the intended effect). Ralph must not write automated tests to verify prompt content or config values.
acceptance_criteria: Ralph does not write automated tests for system prompt or config changes. Ralph does manually verify the behavior works after changes.

title: Update Ralphy's system prompt to know about bd dep add --type relates-to
description: Ralphy's system prompt currently doesn't mention the 'relates-to' dependency type in beads. Add a note that Ralphy should use 'bd dep add --type relates-to' to link issues that could collide (e.g. touch the same files, same page, same GitHub API) without blocking them. This helps concurrent Ralph agents avoid conflicts.
acceptance_criteria: Ralphy uses relates-to links when creating issues that could collide with each other.
close_reason: Added bd dep add --type relates-to to Ralphy's system prompt with TDD tests

title: Update ralph.py to use Kimi K2 Thinking via OpenCode Zen
description: ralph.py currently hardcodes 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-6' as the model. Update it to use Kimi K2 Thinking via OpenCode Zen. The model ID for OpenCode Zen's Kimi K2 Thinking should be verified against the OpenCode Zen model list. Also update ralph_template.py with the same model.
acceptance_criteria: ralph.py and ralph_template.py both use Kimi K2 Thinking via OpenCode Zen as the model. The loop runs successfully with the new model.
notes: Run '/models' in opencode to find the exact model ID for Kimi K2 Thinking on OpenCode Zen.

title: Use ralph_template.py when setting up new user projects
description: When a new project is created, copy ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ralph_template.py to ~/projects/<repo_name>/ralph.py instead of ralph.py. This ensures new projects start with the clean generic Ralph loop, not the justralph.it-specific version.
acceptance_criteria: New project directories contain a ralph.py copied from ralph_template.py, not from ralph.py.

title: Force stop Ralph loop with hard reset
description: Add a 'Force Stop' button on the project page alongside the graceful 'Stop' button, only visible when Ralph is running. Clicking it shows a confirmation dialog: 'This will immediately kill Ralph and hard reset the repo to match origin. Any uncommitted changes will be lost. Are you sure?' If confirmed: (1) immediately kill the ralph.py process (SIGKILL), (2) run 'git reset --hard origin/main && git clean -fd' in the project directory, (3) update DB to ralph_running=false, (4) show 'Force stopped and reset to origin' in the Terminal tab.
acceptance_criteria: Force Stop kills ralph.py immediately. After confirmation, the repo is hard reset to origin. DB reflects stopped state. User sees confirmation dialog before anything happens.

title: Create ralph_template.py: virgin generic Ralph loop
description: Create ralph_template.py in this repo — a clean, generic version of the Ralph loop with no justralph.it-specific customizations. This file is copied to new user project directories as their ralph.py. ralph.py in this repo continues to be the justralph.it-specific version that Ralph may tune freely. ralph_template.py must never be modified by Ralph for justralph.it purposes — it is the canonical starting point for all new projects.
acceptance_criteria: ralph_template.py exists in the repo, is generic (no justralph.it-specific code), and is used as the source when setting up new user projects. ralph.py remains the justralph.it-specific version.

title: Update Ralph's prompt: docs don't need TDD
description: Ralph's system prompt currently instructs it to always follow TDD principles. Add a clarification: TDD (automated tests) applies to code only — documentation (README, AGENTS.md, comments, etc.) does not need automated tests. However, Ralph must still manually verify that documentation is correct and complete after making changes. Additionally, any existing automated tests that only verify documentation content (e.g. checking README contains certain keywords) must be removed.
acceptance_criteria: Ralph does not write automated tests for documentation tasks. Ralph does manually verify docs are correct. Any existing doc-only automated tests are deleted.

title: Adapt Ralphy system prompt for web context
description: Ralphy's system prompt is this repo's OpenCode system prompt, adapted for the web context. Key behaviors to add on top of the existing prompt: (1) call 'show_just_ralph_it_button' tool when spec is complete, (2) after a HUMAN_NEEDED stop, when the user says 'done,' call 'show_just_ralph_it_button' again to resume the loop, (3) whenever the user asks for a recap of issues or progress, always run 'bd list' first to get live data — never recap from memory, (4) Ralphy can only create new beads issues or update issues that are open and not yet claimed — never modify in-progress or completed issues, (5) rigor must be maintained throughout the entire conversation, not just at the start — never accept vague answers, never move on with gaps. Additionally, the web app must inject a <system_reminder> block into every user message server-side (invisible to the user). It must be as concise as possible to avoid context bloat — a few lines max — containing only the most critical behavioral reminders: maintain rigor, never accept vague answers, re-read AGENTS.md and run bd list if context feels stale. The adapted prompt must be set as the system prompt for the opencode server running in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.
acceptance_criteria: Ralphy maintains rigor throughout long conversations. Every user message has a server-side system_reminder injected. After compaction, Ralphy re-anchors from AGENTS.md and bd list before continuing.
close_reason: Adapted Ralphy system prompt for web context: enabled show_just_ralph_it_button tool, added Phase 4 button call, HUMAN_NEEDED resume behavior, and bd list recap constraint

title: Document how to build, run, and test the project
description: Ralph must document the full setup and development workflow in a README.md. It must be as concise as possible — only what's necessary to get the project running from a fresh clone. Cover: (1) prerequisites, (2) environment variables (.env), (3) how to run locally, (4) how to run tests, (5) how to deploy. No fluff, no repetition. This applies to justralph.it itself AND must be added to Ralph's system prompt so it documents every project it builds.
acceptance_criteria: A developer can clone the repo, follow the README, and have the app running without prior knowledge of the VPS state. The README is concise — no unnecessary sections.

title: Bug: secret detection hook triggers false positives on example patterns
description: The git pre-commit secret detection hook rejects commits that contain example secret patterns in issue descriptions (e.g. 'API_KEY=sk-super-secret-123' used as an example in a beads issue JSON). The hook needs to be smarter — it should only flag secrets in actual code/config files, not in .beads/ JSON files or documentation.
acceptance_criteria: Commits containing example secret patterns in .beads/ JSON files or markdown docs are not rejected. Real secrets in code or config files are still caught.
close_reason: Fixed in just-ralph-it-028: .beads/ files are now excluded from secret pattern scanning

title: Render Ralphy chat messages as markdown
description: Ralphy's responses in the chat panel are markdown-formatted. They must be rendered as HTML (bold, italics, lists, code blocks, links, etc.), not displayed as raw markdown text. User messages are plain text and do not need markdown rendering.
acceptance_criteria: Ralphy's responses render markdown correctly in the chat panel. Code blocks, lists, bold/italic text all display as formatted HTML. User messages appear as plain text.

title: Security: prevent .env from being committed
description: Add a git pre-commit hook that rejects commits containing .env files or common secret patterns (e.g. lines matching API_KEY=, SECRET=, PASSWORD=, PRIVATE_KEY=). The hook should be installed automatically as part of the project setup.
acceptance_criteria: Attempting to commit a .env file or a file containing secret-like patterns is rejected with a clear error message.
close_reason: Implemented pre-commit security hook that rejects .env files and secret patterns. Hook auto-installed via setup.sh. 20 tests passing.

title: Security: auto-logout after 30 days of inactivity
description: Sessions expire after 30 days of inactivity. If a user has not made any request in 30 days, their session is invalidated and they are redirected to '/' on their next visit.
acceptance_criteria: A session inactive for 30 days is invalidated. The user is redirected to '/' and must sign in again.

title: Security: HSTS header
description: Add a Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header to all responses: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains'. This ensures browsers always use HTTPS for justralph.it after the first visit.
acceptance_criteria: All responses include the HSTS header. Visiting justralph.it over HTTP redirects to HTTPS.

title: Issues tab must be read-only for users
description: The Issues tab must be strictly read-only for users. Users cannot create, edit, or delete issues from the UI. All issue management goes through Ralphy's chat. If bdui exposes any interactive controls (edit buttons, status toggles, etc.), they must be hidden or disabled. The only way a user affects issues is by chatting with Ralphy.
acceptance_criteria: No interactive issue controls are visible or functional in the Issues tab. Attempting to edit an issue via the UI is not possible.
close_reason: Implemented: bdui bound to localhost, WebSocket proxy filters write operations, frontend connects through proxy. 12 tests cover the changes. All 327 tests pass.

title: Bug: prefers-color-scheme not applied globally
description: The CSS prefers-color-scheme media query is only present in one template (projects/show.html) instead of being applied globally. All pages must respect the system theme. This should be in a single shared CSS file or base template, not duplicated per page.
acceptance_criteria: All pages switch between light and dark mode based on the system theme. No page is stuck in one mode regardless of system preference.
close_reason: Fixed: Created global CSS file (app/static/style.css) with CSS custom properties and prefers-color-scheme light mode media query. Updated base.html to link the stylesheet. Replaced all hardcoded dark-mode colors in templates and JavaScript with CSS variables. All 315 tests pass.

title: Logout
description: Add a logout button (e.g. in the nav or user menu). Clicking it clears the server-side session and redirects to '/'.
acceptance_criteria: Clicking logout clears the session and redirects to the landing page. The user must sign in again to access /projects.
close_reason: Added logout button in nav bar. Clicking it navigates to /auth/logout which clears the session and redirects to /. Tests verify the button appears for authenticated users and is hidden for unauthenticated users.

title: ralph.py: per-iteration log files + main log
description: ralph.py should write logs to two places simultaneously: (1) a per-iteration log file named ralph_<timestamp>_<issue_id>.log (e.g. ralph_2026-03-15T18:30:00_just-ralph-it-123.log) in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/logs/, created fresh for each issue, (2) ralph.log in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/, appended to on every run — this is the file the Terminal tab in the web UI streams. Both files must be written with flush=True (or PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1) for real-time streaming.
acceptance_criteria: Each ralph.py issue run produces a new timestamped log file in logs/. ralph.log accumulates all output across all runs. Both update in real-time, not after the run finishes.
close_reason: Implemented per-iteration log files + main log. LOG_FILE moved to project root, RotatingFileHandler replaced with plain FileHandler in append mode, per-iteration logs created as ralph_<timestamp>_<issue_id>.log in logs/, both files flushed in real-time. All 292 tests pass.

title: Stop and continue Ralph loop from web UI
description: Add stop/continue controls to the project page. When Ralph is running, show a 'Stop' button. Clicking it creates ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.stop (or ~/projects/<project_name>/.stop for per-project VPS), which causes ralph.py to exit cleanly after the current issue finishes. The UI polls or listens for the process to exit and then shows a 'Continue' button. Clicking 'Continue' spawns ralph.py again from the project directory, resuming from the next ready issue. The 'Ralph is building...' status indicator updates accordingly: running → stopping (after .stop created, before process exits) → stopped → running again.
acceptance_criteria: User can stop a running Ralph loop from the UI. The loop finishes the current issue before stopping. User can resume the loop from the UI. Status indicator reflects the current state accurately.
close_reason: Fixed stop/continue feature: aligned stdout format in ralph.py (bare %(message)s for stdout, full format for file), fixed reason detection in routes.py and recovery.py to match STOPPING AS REQUESTED constant, updated test mocks. All 66 related tests pass.

title: ralph.py: graceful stop via .stop flag file
description: Add a graceful stop mechanism to ralph.py. At the top of each loop iteration, before claiming the next issue, ralph.py checks if ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.stop exists. If it does, ralph.py deletes the file and exits cleanly with a message: 'Stopping as requested.' This allows the user to stop the loop between issues without interrupting a running session. The check must happen AFTER the current issue finishes and BEFORE picking up the next one.
acceptance_criteria: Creating a .stop file in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ causes ralph.py to exit cleanly after the current issue finishes. The .stop file is deleted on exit. No issue is left in a broken state.
close_reason: Implemented graceful stop via .stop flag file. Check at top of loop before claiming next issue. File is deleted on exit. 6 tests added and passing.

title: Set up beads JSONL export for cross-machine sync
description: Configure beads to export issues to .beads/issues.jsonl after every change, so the issues are accessible via the GitHub repo and can be imported on other machines (e.g. the author's Mac) via git pull. Set up a git hook (post-commit or via bd hooks) that runs 'bd export > .beads/issues.jsonl' and commits the file. Ensure .beads/issues.jsonl is tracked by git (not in .gitignore). On git pull, bd should automatically import from the JSONL if it's newer than the local DB.
acceptance_criteria: After any beads change, issues.jsonl is updated and committed to the repo. Running git pull on another machine (e.g. Mac) and then bd list shows the latest issues.

title: Bug: app name displayed as 'just-ralph-it' instead of 'justralph.it'
description: The UI is displaying the app name as 'just-ralph-it' (the repo name) instead of 'justralph.it' (the brand name). All user-facing references to the app name should use 'justralph.it'.
acceptance_criteria: The app name appears as 'justralph.it' everywhere in the UI.
close_reason: Fixed: all user-facing UI now shows 'justralph.it' instead of 'just-ralph-it'. Changed 7 files: base.html title, index.html h1, show.html title, new.html title, manifest.json name+short_name, sw.js notification title, and updated test assertion.

title: Process recovery on startup
description: When the web app starts, it reads the SQLite DB and: (1) restarts the bdui sidecar for every project on its stored port, (2) for any project where ralph_running=true in the DB, resumes ralph.py (spawns it with cwd=~/projects/<project_name>/). The DB must have a ralph_running boolean column on the projects table, set to true when ralph.py is spawned and false when it exits (any exit state). This ensures crash recovery after VPS reboots.
acceptance_criteria: After a VPS reboot, all bdui sidecars are running and any previously-running Ralph loop resumes automatically without manual intervention.

title: Ralphy 'show Just Ralph It button' custom tool
description: Create a global opencode custom tool at ~/.config/opencode/tools/show_just_ralph_it_button.ts. When Ralphy calls this tool, it makes an HTTP POST to the web app's internal endpoint (e.g. POST /internal/projects/:slug/show-button), which pushes a 'Just Ralph It' button into the chat UI via SSE. The tool receives the project slug as an argument so the web app knows which project's chat to update. The button only appears when Ralph is not currently running — if Ralph is already running, the tool call is a no-op. Ralphy may call this tool multiple times: once when the spec is complete, and again after a HUMAN_NEEDED stop to resume the loop.
acceptance_criteria: When Ralphy calls the tool and Ralph is not running, the 'Just Ralph It' button appears in the chat UI for the correct project. Calling it while Ralph is already running has no effect.
notes: If stuck: see https://opencode.ai/docs/custom-tools.md. The tool is a TypeScript file in ~/.config/opencode/tools/. Use Bun fetch to call the web app's internal endpoint.

title: Ralphy opencode server startup
description: The Ralphy opencode server must run persistently in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ via 'opencode serve'. It handles all Ralphy sessions (one per user project). Start it as a separate systemd service (not a subprocess of the web app) so it survives web app restarts and restarts automatically on crash. The web app communicates with it at its base URL (e.g. http://127.0.0.1:4096), which must be configured as an environment variable.
acceptance_criteria: The opencode server is running and reachable at its base URL when the web app starts. GET /global/health returns healthy. If the server crashes, systemd restarts it automatically.
notes: If stuck: run 'opencode serve' in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/. Check https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md for options.

title: Production deployment
description: Deploy the web app to justralph.it. There is an existing systemd service serving a static HTML file on this VPS — find it, stop it, and replace it with the new web app. The web app must run on port 80/443, be managed by systemd, and restart automatically on crash. TLS should be handled (Let's Encrypt or existing cert).
acceptance_criteria: justralph.it serves the web app over HTTPS. The systemd service restarts the app automatically on crash. The old static HTML service is gone.
notes: Prerequisites (must be done manually before Ralph deploys): (1) Create a GitHub OAuth App at github.com/settings/developers with callback URL https://justralph.it/auth/callback, then set GITHUB_CLIENT_ID and GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET as environment variables on the VPS. If stuck: check existing systemd services with 'systemctl list-units --type=service' and look for anything serving port 80/443.

title: Terminal tab: Ralph stdout streaming
description: The 'Terminal' tab streams stdout from the running ralph.py process in real-time. Output is displayed as plain text in a terminal-style view (dark background, monospace font). Before Ralph has ever run, shows a placeholder: 'When the Ralph loop starts, you'll see its stdout here'. When ralph.py exits with ALL_DONE, shows 'Ralph is done.' When it exits with HUMAN_NEEDED, Ralphy sends a chat message saying 'Ralph is blocked — check the Issues tab' and the right panel automatically switches to the Issues tab.
acceptance_criteria: Terminal tab shows ralph.py output line by line as it runs. ALL_DONE shows completion message. HUMAN_NEEDED triggers a Ralphy chat message and switches to Issues tab.

title: 'Just Ralph It' button and Ralph loop launch
description: Ralphy has a tool it can call to render a 'Just Ralph It' button in the chat when it determines the spec is complete. When the user clicks the button: (1) the server spawns ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ralph.py as a subprocess with cwd=~/projects/<project_name>/, (2) stdout is streamed in real-time to the Terminal tab, (3) the button disappears and is replaced by a 'Ralph is building...' status indicator.
acceptance_criteria: Ralphy can trigger the button via a tool call. Clicking it starts ralph.py, streams output to Terminal tab, and shows a running status. The button is gone while Ralph is running.
notes: If stuck: ralph.py is already in this repo. Spawn it as a subprocess with cwd set to the project directory. Stream stdout line-by-line to the client via WebSocket or SSE.

title: Issues tab: beads visualization
description: The 'Issues' tab shows a functional, read-only issue visualization for the project's beads issues. A bdui sidecar process is started per project when the project is created, on a unique port (stored in the SQLite DB alongside other project fields). The tab fetches issue data from that bdui server's HTTP/WebSocket API. Displays issues with their status (open, in-progress, completed, blocked). Updates live as issues change. No styling requirements — just functional. If no issues exist yet, shows a placeholder: 'Continue chatting to let Ralphy create the spec'. The user cannot create, edit, or delete issues from this tab — all issue management goes through Ralphy's chat.
acceptance_criteria: Issues tab shows all beads issues for the project. Issue statuses update in real-time as Ralphy creates issues or Ralph works on them.
notes: If stuck: start bdui with 'bdui start --host 0.0.0.0 --port <port>' in the project directory. Pick an available port and store it in the DB. Check beads-ui's HTTP/WebSocket API for fetching issue data.

title: Spec tab: live AGENTS.md viewer
description: The 'Spec' tab on the right panel reads and displays the contents of ~/projects/<project_name>/AGENTS.md. Updates in real-time as Ralphy writes to the file (poll or file-watch). Renders as formatted markdown. If AGENTS.md does not exist yet, shows a placeholder message: 'Continue chatting to let Ralphy create the spec'.
acceptance_criteria: AGENTS.md content appears in the Spec tab. When Ralphy updates the file during the interview, the tab reflects the new content within a few seconds without a page reload.

title: Ralphy chat interface
description: Wire up the chat panel to the project's opencode session on the Ralphy server. The Ralphy opencode server runs in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ via 'opencode serve'. Each project has its own session ID stored in the DB. When the user lands on /projects/:slug for the first time, send the project description as the first message via POST /session/:id/prompt_async. Subsequent user messages are sent the same way. The browser subscribes to the SSE stream (GET /event on the opencode server, proxied through the web app backend, filtered by session ID) to receive Ralphy's streaming responses. Chat history is loaded on page open via GET /session/:id/message. Ralphy can only create new beads issues or update issues that are open and not yet claimed — it must not modify in-progress or completed issues.
acceptance_criteria: Chat messages send and receive in real-time. First message is pre-populated with the project description. Reopening the browser shows full chat history. Ralphy cannot modify claimed or closed issues.
notes: If stuck: opencode server API at https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md. Use POST /session/:id/prompt_async to send messages (returns 204 immediately). Use GET /event (SSE) to stream responses. Use GET /session/:id/message to load history.

title: Project page layout
description: Build the '/projects/:slug' route shell. Two-panel layout: chat panel on the left, tabbed panel on the right with three tabs: 'Spec' (shows AGENTS.md content), 'Issues' (beads visualization placeholder), 'Terminal' (Ralph stdout placeholder). Requires authentication. The chat panel is always visible regardless of project state.
acceptance_criteria: Navigating to /projects/:slug shows the two-panel layout with all three tabs. Switching tabs works. Unauthenticated user is redirected to '/'.

title: New project form and setup
description: Build the '/projects/new' route. Shows a form with two fields: 'Repo name' (GitHub-valid characters only: alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, dots — enforced client-side) and 'Description' (placeholder: 'What do you want to build?'). On submit, the server: (1) validates the repo name is not already taken as a GitHub repo or local directory ~/projects/<repo_name>/ — if taken, rejects with an error, (2) creates a new GitHub repo named after the repo name field using the user's GitHub App installation token, (3) clones it to ~/projects/<repo_name>/ on the VPS, (4) initializes beads in that directory (with BEADS_DOLT_SHARED_SERVER=1, each project gets its own Dolt DB on the shared server), (5) copies ralph_template.py to ~/projects/<repo_name>/ralph.py, (6) starts a bdui sidecar process for this project on a unique available port (stored in the DB), (7) creates a new opencode session via POST /session on the Ralphy opencode server (running in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ via 'opencode serve'), storing the returned session ID, (8) stores the project in the SQLite database (name, slug, github_repo_url, vps_path, opencode_session_id, bdui_port), (9) redirects to /projects/:slug.
acceptance_criteria: Submitting the form creates the GitHub repo, clones it on the VPS, sets up all project files, starts the Ralphy session, and redirects to /projects/:slug. The project appears in /projects.
notes: If stuck: the Ralphy opencode server runs in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/ via 'opencode serve'. Create a session per project via POST /session. The opencode server API is documented at https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md.

title: Projects list page
description: Build the '/projects' route. Shows all projects belonging to the authenticated user. Each project shows its name and links to '/projects/:slug'. Includes a 'New Project' button linking to '/projects/new'. Requires authentication — unauthenticated users are redirected to '/'.
acceptance_criteria: Authenticated user sees their projects listed. 'New Project' button is present. Unauthenticated user is redirected to '/'.

title: Landing page
description: Build the '/' route. Shows the app name/description and a 'Sign in with GitHub' button. Unauthenticated users land here. Authenticated users are redirected to /projects.
acceptance_criteria: Unauthenticated user sees landing page with sign-in button. Authenticated user visiting '/' is redirected to /projects.

title: GitHub App authentication
description: Implement GitHub App-based authentication. Create a GitHub App with permissions: repository (read/write) for creating repos and pushing code. Implement the GitHub App installation flow: user clicks 'Sign in with GitHub', gets redirected to install/authorize the GitHub App, callback stores the installation token server-side. If the authenticated GitHub username is not 'nicopujia', reject and show: 'Not available yet.' Use the installation token for all subsequent GitHub API calls (repo creation, pushes).
acceptance_criteria: Signing in with GitHub username 'nicopujia' succeeds and stores the installation token. Any other GitHub account sees 'Not available yet.' error. The token can be used to create repos and push code.
notes: Credentials are already on disk: GITHUB_APP_ID and GITHUB_CLIENT_ID in ~/projects/just-ralph-it/.env, private key at ~/projects/just-ralph-it/just-ralph-it.2026-03-14.private-key.pem. Load these at runtime — do NOT commit them. See https://docs.github.com/en/apps/creating-github-apps/about-creating-github-apps/about-creating-github-apps

title: Project scaffolding and dev environment
description: Set up the web app project: choose stack (Python backend, HTMX frontend, SQLite database), initialize repo structure, configure dev server, linter, and basic tooling. Also install beads-ui globally: 'npm i beads-ui -g'. UI should be functional only — no styling requirements. Ralph decides the rest, keeping it simple.
acceptance_criteria: Dev server runs locally, project structure is in place, linter passes.

title: Fix test_ralph_claim: test expects --claim flag but code uses --status in_progress
UI: center page content except on project view
description: All pages except /projects/:slug should have their content centered horizontally with a max-width container (e.g. max-width: 800px, margin: 0 auto). The project view (/projects/:slug) stays full-width to accommodate the two-panel layout.
acceptance_criteria: Landing page, /projects, /projects/new, /pricing all have centered content with a max-width. /projects/:slug remains full-width.

title: Clear Terminal tab and ralph.py stdout at start of each issue
description: At the start of each issue iteration in ralph.py: (1) print a clear separator/header in stdout (e.g. a line of dashes + issue ID + timestamp) so the Terminal tab visually separates each issue's output, (2) the Terminal tab in the web UI should also scroll to the bottom and visually indicate a new issue has started. Optionally clear previous output or just add a clear visual separator.
acceptance_criteria: Each new issue in the Terminal tab starts with a clear visual separator showing the issue ID. Output from different issues is easy to distinguish.

title: UI improvements: project creation and project page header
description: Several UI improvements: (1) /projects/new: support file attachments in the description field (user can attach files alongside their description text), (2) /projects/new: add small helper text 'Markdown supported' below the description field, (3) /projects/new: add a 'Back to projects' button in the page header linking to /projects, (4) /projects/:slug: replace any 'Back to projects' button with a breadcrumb in the header: 'All projects / [project name]' where 'All projects' links to /projects and '[project name]' is plain text.
acceptance_criteria: New project form has file attachment support, 'Markdown supported' hint, and 'Back to projects' header button. Project page header shows 'All projects / [project name]' breadcrumb with 'All projects' linking to /projects.

title: Ralphy system prompt live reload on update
description: When Ralphy's system prompt file is updated in this repo, all existing opencode sessions should pick up the new prompt automatically. Implement a git post-push hook that: (1) checks if the system prompt file was changed in the pushed commits, (2) if so, uses opencode's import functionality to reload the prompt into all active Ralphy sessions via the opencode server API. The hook must only trigger on system prompt file changes — not on every push.
acceptance_criteria: Pushing a commit that changes the system prompt file triggers a reload of all active Ralphy sessions. Pushing commits that don't touch the system prompt file does not trigger any reload.
notes: Check opencode's import/reload API at https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md. Use 'git diff HEAD~1 HEAD --name-only' in the hook to detect which files changed.

title: Asset file size cap and subagent reading
description: Enforce the following rules for file attachments in Ralphy's chat: (1) Size cap: files larger than 5MB are rejected at upload time with an error message. (2) Readable types: only images, PDFs, and text-based files (e.g. .txt, .md, .json, .csv) can be read by Ralphy. Audio and video files are stored on the VPS but never read by Ralphy. (3) Subagent reading: when Ralphy needs to read a file's contents (e.g. a wireframe), it must spawn a subagent to read and summarize the file, then report back a concise, spec-relevant summary to Ralphy's main context. Ralphy never reads files directly.
acceptance_criteria: Files over 5MB are rejected at upload. Audio/video files are stored but never read. When Ralphy reads a file, it uses a subagent and receives a summary, not raw contents.

title: Secret injection via .env in web UI
description: When Ralph files a HUMAN_NEEDED issue requiring a secret (e.g. a Stripe API key), the web UI should provide a simple way for the user to inject it. On the project page, add a 'Add secret' input (key name + value) that appends the secret to ~/projects/<project_name>/.env on the VPS. Ralph's system prompt must tell it: 'Any secrets you need will be in ~/projects/<project_name>/.env. If a required secret is missing, file a HUMAN_NEEDED issue specifying the exact env var name needed.'
acceptance_criteria: User can add a secret via the web UI. It appears in .env on the VPS. Ralph's prompt instructs it to always look in .env for secrets and request missing ones by exact env var name via HUMAN_NEEDED.

title: Asset file handling: upload to VPS and reference in spec
description: When a user attaches a file in the Ralphy chat, it is saved to ~/projects/<project_name>/assets/ on the VPS. Ralphy receives only the filename in its context by default — not the file contents. Ralphy decides when to explicitly read a file (e.g. a wireframe that informs a UI issue). When Ralphy references an asset in a beads issue, it includes the path (e.g. assets/wireframe.png) so Ralph knows where to find it on the VPS.
acceptance_criteria: Attached files appear in ~/projects/<project_name>/assets/. Ralphy's context shows only the filename, not the contents. Ralphy can choose to read a file when relevant. Beads issues reference asset paths correctly.

title: Landing page: show 'Go to dashboard' for logged-in users
description: When an authenticated user visits '/', do not redirect them to /projects. Instead, show the landing page normally but replace the 'Sign in with GitHub' button with a 'Go to dashboard' button that links to /projects.
acceptance_criteria: An authenticated user visiting '/' sees the landing page with a 'Go to dashboard' button instead of 'Sign in with GitHub'. No redirect occurs.

title: Project deletion: confirm by typing project name
description: Before deleting a project, show a confirmation dialog that requires the user to type the project's repo name exactly. The delete button is disabled until the typed name matches. Also includes the optional GitHub repo deletion checkbox (default: unchecked). Only when the name matches and the user clicks confirm does deletion proceed.
acceptance_criteria: The delete button is disabled until the user types the exact project name. Typing the wrong name keeps the button disabled. Correct name enables it. Deletion only proceeds on confirm.

title: Redirect /prd to https://nicolaspujia.com/ralph
description: The /prd route must redirect (301) to https://nicolaspujia.com/ralph. This was previously served as a static HTML file and should be restored in the new web app.
acceptance_criteria: Navigating to justralph.it/prd redirects to https://nicolaspujia.com/ralph.

title: Browser tab titles
description: Set the HTML <title> tag for every page to follow the format '[page name] • justralph.it'. Examples: 'Projects • justralph.it', 'New Project • justralph.it', '<project name> • justralph.it', 'Pricing • justralph.it'. Exception: the home page title is just 'justralph.it'.
acceptance_criteria: Every page has a correct, descriptive browser tab title following the format '[page name] • justralph.it'.

title: Fix tests that mock subprocess.run but not subprocess.Popen
description: 7 tests in TestMainReloadsAfterDone (4 tests) and test_ralph_claim.py (3 tests) hang or fail because they only mock subprocess.run, but ralph.py uses subprocess.Popen to launch opencode. These tests need to also mock subprocess.Popen like the TestGracefulStop class already does correctly. Discovered while fixing just-ralph-it-wc6.
close_reason: Fixed all 7 tests: added @patch("ralph.subprocess.Popen") to 4 tests in TestMainReloadsAfterDone and 3 tests in test_ralph_claim.py. Switched to _make_run_side_effect_for_popen_tests + _make_mock_popen helpers. All 272 tests pass.

title: Fix pre-existing test failures in test_ralph.py and test_ralph_claim.py
description: 12 tests in test_ralph.py and test_ralph_claim.py fail due to 3 root causes: (1) sudo -n systemctl mismatch - tests expect ['systemctl', 'reload', ...] but code uses ['sudo', '-n', 'systemctl', 'reload', ...'], (2) argparse picks up pytest's -v flag - tests calling main() fail with SystemExit: 2, (3) stderr vs stdout for logging - test_stop_file_prints_stopping_message captures stdout but logger.info goes to stderr via StreamHandler.
close_reason: Fixed all 3 root causes: (1) Updated all test assertions/filters to expect sudo -n systemctl instead of just systemctl, (2) Added @patch sys.argv decorators to 7 tests calling main(), (3) Changed StreamHandler() to StreamHandler(sys.stdout) in ralph.py

title: Project deletion: optional GitHub repo deletion
description: Update the project deletion flow to include a checkbox (default: unchecked) asking 'Also delete the GitHub repository'. If unchecked, deleting the project removes it from justralph.it (VPS directory, processes, DB record) but leaves the GitHub repo intact. If checked, the GitHub repo is also deleted via the GitHub API. The confirmation dialog should make this choice clear.
acceptance_criteria: Deleting a project with the checkbox unchecked leaves the GitHub repo intact. Deleting with the checkbox checked removes the GitHub repo. Default is unchecked.

title: File attachments in Ralphy chat
description: Allow the user to attach files (images, audio, wireframes, etc.) in the Ralphy chat panel. The user can drag-and-drop or use a file picker button. Attached files are sent as parts in the message to the Ralphy opencode session via POST /session/:id/message or /session/:id/prompt_async. Opencode natively supports image scanning — check if the HTTP API's 'parts' field supports file/image parts and use that.
acceptance_criteria: User can attach an image or file to a chat message. The file is sent to Ralphy's opencode session and Ralphy can reference its contents in its response.
notes: If stuck: check the opencode HTTP API message 'parts' schema at https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md and the types at the SDK types URL. Opencode supports images natively in the TUI via drag-and-drop — the API likely has an equivalent.

title: Browser push notifications for Ralph loop events
description: When the user clicks 'Just Ralph It', request browser push notification permission. If granted, subscribe the browser to push notifications via the Web Push API and store the subscription server-side. When ralph.py exits with HUMAN_NEEDED or ALL_DONE, the server sends a push notification to the stored subscription. HUMAN_NEEDED message: 'Ralph is blocked and needs your help.' ALL_DONE message: 'Ralph is done building your project.' If the user denies permission, silently skip — no error shown.
acceptance_criteria: After clicking 'Just Ralph It' and granting permission, the user receives a browser push notification when the loop ends with HUMAN_NEEDED or ALL_DONE, even if the browser tab is closed.
notes: If stuck: use the Web Push protocol (pywebpush library for Python backend). Store the push subscription in the SQLite DB per project.

title: Automatic Ralphy session compaction
description: Monitor the Ralphy opencode session size for each project. When the session approaches the context limit, automatically call POST /session/:id/summarize on the Ralphy opencode server. This should happen silently in the background — no user notification. Ralphy can recover full context from the filesystem (AGENTS.md and beads issues) after compaction. Trigger compaction aggressively (e.g. when session reaches 80% of context limit).
acceptance_criteria: When a Ralphy session grows large, it is automatically summarized without user intervention. After compaction, Ralphy continues to function correctly, reading context from the filesystem as needed.
notes: If stuck: use GET /session/:id to check session size/token count. Use POST /session/:id/summarize to compact. See https://opencode.ai/docs/server.md.

title: Project deletion
description: Add a delete button on the project page (or /projects list). When confirmed: (1) deletes the GitHub repo via the GitHub API using the user's OAuth token, (2) removes ~/projects/<project_name>/ from the VPS, (3) stops the Ralphy opencode session (DELETE /session/:id on the Ralphy opencode server), the bdui sidecar, and any running ralph.py process for this project, (4) removes the project from the database, (5) redirects to /projects.
acceptance_criteria: Deleting a project removes the GitHub repo, VPS directory, all running processes for that project, and the DB record. User is redirected to /projects.

title: AI API proxy on central server
description: The AI provider API key never lives on user VPSes. Instead, the central server runs a proxy endpoint (e.g. POST /internal/ai/chat) that accepts opencode-compatible requests from user VPSes, adds the real API key, forwards to the AI provider, and returns the response. User VPSes are configured with the proxy URL and a per-project auth token instead of the real API key. The proxy also records token usage per project for billing. Rate limiting can be applied per user at the proxy level. The specific AI provider (opencode Zen, OpenRouter, etc.) is TBD.
acceptance_criteria: opencode on a user VPS successfully makes AI calls via the proxy. The API key is never present on the VPS. Token usage is recorded per project at the proxy.
notes: Configure opencode on the VPS to use the proxy URL as the API base URL with the per-project auth token. The proxy must be compatible with the OpenAI-compatible API format that opencode expects. AI provider TBD.

title: SSH key generation and user download
description: When a project VPS is provisioned, the central server generates an SSH key pair (ed25519). The public key is installed on the VPS at provisioning time via cloud-init. The private key is stored encrypted in the DB associated with the project. On the project page, show a 'Download SSH key' button that lets the user download their private key as a .pem file. The button is only visible once the VPS is provisioned (i.e. after 'Just Ralph It' has been clicked).
acceptance_criteria: After VPS provisioning, the user can download their SSH private key from the project page. The key works to SSH into the project VPS.
notes: Use ed25519 for the key pair. Encrypt the private key at rest in the DB. Never expose the private key in logs or API responses other than the explicit download endpoint.

title: VPS auto-scaling
description: When creating a project, show an 'Auto-scale VPS' checkbox (default: checked). This setting is stored in the DB per project. At the start of each issue, ralph.py (via controller.py) checks if VPS resources are sufficient (CPU, RAM, disk) using its own judgment. If resources are insufficient: (1) if auto-scale is on: call the Hetzner API to upgrade the VPS to the next available plan, wait for the resize to complete, then continue, (2) if auto-scale is off: fire a browser push notification to the user ('Your project VPS is running low on resources') and continue as best it can. The resource check and scaling decision must be included in ralph.py's per-issue prompt so Ralph is explicitly told to do it.
acceptance_criteria: With auto-scale on, Ralph upgrades the VPS plan when needed without user intervention. With auto-scale off, the user gets a push notification instead. The resource check happens at the start of every issue.
notes: If stuck: use Hetzner's server action API to resize (POST /servers/{id}/actions/change_type). The next plan is the one above the current in Hetzner's plan list.

title: Shared real-time group chat
description: The Ralphy chat on a shared project is a single group chat visible to all team members in real-time. Each message displays the sender's GitHub username and avatar. All members can send messages. New messages from any member appear instantly for all other members via SSE. Chat history shows all messages from all members.
acceptance_criteria: Two team members with the project open simultaneously both see each other's messages in real-time. Each message shows the sender's username. Chat history is complete for all members.

title: Ralphy multi-user awareness
description: Update Ralphy's system prompt to be aware of multi-user sessions. Ralphy receives each message with the sender's GitHub username prepended (e.g. '[nicopujia]: I want the app to use Python'). Ralphy addresses users by username when relevant. When two members contradict each other, Ralphy explicitly surfaces the conflict ('nicopujia said X, but user B said Y — which should it be?') and waits for resolution before continuing. Ralphy does not proceed past a contradiction.
acceptance_criteria: Ralphy correctly identifies who sent each message. When two users contradict each other, Ralphy flags it and asks for resolution. Ralphy does not silently pick one side.

title: Team management
description: Allow users to create teams and invite members. A user can: (1) create a team with a name, (2) invite other users by GitHub username — invitees receive a notification and can accept or decline, (3) remove members, (4) delete the team. Team membership is stored in the DB. A user can be in multiple teams.
acceptance_criteria: A user can create a team, invite another user by GitHub username, and the invitee can accept. Both users appear as team members. The inviting user can remove members and delete the team.

title: Project sharing with teams
description: A project owner can add their project to a team. All team members then have access to the project view (/projects/:slug). Non-members cannot access the project. The project owner retains full control (can remove the project from the team, delete it, etc.). A project can only belong to one team at a time.
acceptance_criteria: After a project owner adds a project to a team, all team members can navigate to /projects/:slug and see the full project view. Non-team members get a 403.

title: Multi-user collaboration
description: Allow users to collaborate on projects via teams. Features: (1) Team management: a user can create a team, invite other users by GitHub username, and members can accept/decline invites. (2) Project sharing: a project owner can add a project to a team, giving all team members access to the project view. (3) Shared chat: the Ralphy chat is a single group chat visible to all team members. Each message shows the sender's GitHub username. All members can send messages. (4) Ralphy behavior: Ralphy is aware of who is sending each message (addressed by username). When team members contradict each other, Ralphy surfaces the conflict explicitly and asks the team to resolve it before continuing. Ralphy treats the team as a single client and is the authority on resolving ambiguity.
acceptance_criteria: Two users in the same team can both see and send messages in a shared project chat. Ralphy addresses users by name and flags contradictions. The right panel (Spec/Issues/Terminal) is shared and updates for all members in real-time.

title: Ralph: legacy repo analysis and spec generation
description: Ralph picks up the 'Analyze legacy repo and generate spec' issue. Steps: (1) clone the legacy repo to a temp directory (e.g. /tmp/<project_name>_legacy/), (2) spawn hundreds or thousands of subagents to analyze the codebase exhaustively — every route, every API endpoint, every data model, every user flow, every edge case, every error state, every config option, every integration. Nothing can be skipped or assumed. If the user wants a clean rebuild with the same behavior, 100% behavioral coverage is mandatory — a missing feature is a bug, (3) create beads issues in the new project directory that fully describe the behavior to rebuild, incorporating the user's desired changes from AGENTS.md. Each issue must be atomic and testable. Acceptance criteria must be verifiable against the original behavior, (4) delete the temp clone, (5) close the analysis issue only when every observable behavior of the legacy codebase has a corresponding beads issue. Ralph then continues the loop with the newly created issues.
acceptance_criteria: After Ralph closes the analysis issue, every observable behavior of the legacy codebase has a corresponding beads issue. A QA pass comparing the rebuilt app against the original should find zero missing features. The legacy repo temp clone is deleted.
notes: If stuck: use subagents aggressively — one per module, one per API endpoint, one per data model, one per user flow, etc. When in doubt, create an issue. It is better to over-spec than under-spec. Do not close this issue until you are certain nothing has been missed.

title: Ralphy: legacy repo interview mode
description: When a project has a legacy repo URL, Ralphy's interview focuses on understanding what the user wants different in the rebuild (could be nothing — just a clean rebuild with a new stack). Ralphy must understand the delta between the legacy behavior and the desired new behavior. At the end of the interview, instead of (or in addition to) the normal AGENTS.md + issues, Ralphy creates a single beads issue: 'Analyze legacy repo and generate spec' with the legacy repo URL, the user's desired changes, and instructions for Ralph to clone the repo to a temp directory, spawn subagents to analyze it, create beads issues from the analysis, and delete the temp clone. Ralphy then shows the 'Just Ralph It' button as normal.
acceptance_criteria: For a legacy project, Ralphy produces a single 'analyze repo' issue with all necessary context. The issue is self-contained enough for Ralph to execute without further clarification.

title: Legacy codebase input
description: Allow users to provide an existing GitHub repo URL when creating a new project. The new project form gets an optional 'GitHub repo URL' field. If provided, the description field label changes to 'What do you want different in the rebuild?' (placeholder: 'Nothing — just a clean rebuild'). GitHub login is mandatory for this feature. The new project repo starts fresh (empty), not forked from the legacy repo.
acceptance_criteria: User can create a project with a legacy repo URL. The form adapts accordingly. The new project repo is fresh, not a fork.

title: Google OAuth login and VPS-only projects
description: Allow users without a GitHub account to sign in with Google OAuth. For Google-authenticated users, projects have no GitHub repo — code lives only on the VPS (~/projects/<project_name>/). No repo creation, no auto-push. Project deletion just removes the VPS directory. All other features (Ralphy interview, Ralph loop, beads issues, terminal, spec tab) work the same. GitHub-authenticated users are unaffected.
acceptance_criteria: A user can sign in with Google, create projects, run Ralph loops, and have their code stored on the VPS without any GitHub integration.

title: controller.py: self-update mechanism
description: controller.py must be a single self-contained Python file. It periodically polls the central server (e.g. GET /internal/controller/version) to check if a newer version is available. If so, it downloads the new file, replaces itself on disk, and triggers a systemd restart (systemctl restart controller). The central server serves the latest controller.py at a known endpoint. Version is a simple integer or hash. The update check happens in the background and does not interrupt the current Ralph loop — the restart happens between issues (same safe point as the .stop flag).
acceptance_criteria: When controller.py is updated on the central server, all user VPSes automatically download and restart with the new version within one polling interval. The update never interrupts a mid-issue Ralph loop.
notes: If stuck: use a background thread for the update check. To self-replace: write new content to a temp file, move it over controller.py, then call 'systemctl restart controller'.

title: Pricing page
description: Build a /pricing page that explains the free vs paid tiers and has an 'Upgrade' button that initiates the Stripe checkout flow. Free tier: Ralphy chat only, max 3 projects, no Ralph loop. Pro tier: everything, billed monthly. Pricing constants must be configurable in code (not hardcoded in templates): PLAN_BASE_PRICE=50, VPS_PRICE_PER_PROJECT=10, TOKEN_PRICE_PER_MILLION=30. Pricing displayed: $50/mo base (includes Ralphy usage) + $10/mo per active project + $30/1M tokens (Ralph building usage). Page is accessible to both authenticated and unauthenticated users.
acceptance_criteria: Navigating to /pricing shows the tier comparison with correct prices. Clicking Upgrade redirects to Stripe checkout for authenticated users, or to sign-in for unauthenticated users. Prices are driven by constants, not hardcoded strings.

title: Free tier enforcement
description: Enforce free tier limits: (1) 'Just Ralph It' button is visible for free users but clicking it redirects to /pricing, (2) creating a 4th project redirects to /pricing, (3) beads issues are not exportable for free users — the Issues tab shows issues but there is no export button and the issues.jsonl file is not committed to the GitHub repo for free users' projects. Paid users and 'nicopujia' have no restrictions.
acceptance_criteria: Free user cannot start a Ralph loop, create more than 3 projects, or export their issues. Paid users have full access including issue export.

title: Monthly Stripe invoicing
description: At the end of each user's billing cycle (monthly from their first payment date), calculate the total bill: (1) base fee: $50/mo, (2) per active project VPS: $10/mo each, (3) token usage: total tokens used that month × $30/1M. Charge via Stripe. Pricing constants must match those in the pricing page (PLAN_BASE_PRICE=50, VPS_PRICE_PER_PROJECT=10, TOKEN_PRICE_PER_MILLION=30). If payment fails, block the user's account (no new Ralph loops, no new projects) until payment succeeds. Send a Stripe invoice email automatically.
acceptance_criteria: At billing cycle end, the user is charged the correct amount based on token usage. Failed payments block the account. Successful payments reset the cycle.
notes: If stuck: use Stripe's invoice API or metered subscriptions. Listen for invoice.payment_failed webhook to block accounts.

title: Token usage tracking
description: Track token usage per user per billing cycle. Every time ralph.py runs an opencode session, the token count (input + output) is reported back to the central server via controller.py and stored in the DB (user_id, tokens_used, timestamp). At the end of each billing cycle, total tokens are summed for invoicing.
acceptance_criteria: Token usage is recorded in the DB for every Ralph loop session. BYOK users have no token records. Monthly totals are queryable per user.
notes: If stuck: opencode may expose token usage in its session response or events. Check GET /session/:id for usage metadata.

title: Stripe subscription and checkout
description: Implement Stripe-hosted checkout for upgrading from free to paid tier. When a free user tries to click 'Just Ralph It' or create a 4th project, show an upgrade prompt with a 'Upgrade' button. Clicking it redirects to a Stripe-hosted checkout page. On successful payment, Stripe webhook updates the user's account to 'paid' in the DB and starts their billing cycle from that date. On failed payment or cancellation, user stays on free tier.
acceptance_criteria: Free user clicking 'Just Ralph It' or creating a 4th project sees an upgrade prompt. Completing Stripe checkout upgrades their account. Billing cycle starts from payment date.
notes: If stuck: use Stripe Checkout (hosted page). Listen for checkout.session.completed webhook to confirm payment and activate the account.

title: BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) support
description: Allow users to optionally provide their own opencode Zen API key instead of using justralph.it's default Qwen-based key. In the user settings page, the user can paste their opencode Zen key. If set, it is stored encrypted in the DB and injected into their VPS's opencode config instead of the default key. If not set, the default Qwen key is used and usage is billed via Stripe.
acceptance_criteria: A user with a BYOK opencode Zen key set has their key used for all Ralph loop runs on their VPS. A user without a BYOK key uses the default Qwen key and is billed accordingly.
close_reason: BYOK discarded — adds complexity without revenue benefit

title: controller.py: VPS ↔ central server communication
description: Implement controller.py — a lightweight HTTP server that runs on each user VPS and acts as the communication bridge between the VPS and the central server. Responsibilities: (1) VPS → central: receives events and stdout from ralph.py (via local IPC or file/queue) and forwards them to the central server's internal API in real-time. (2) Central → VPS: exposes an authenticated HTTP endpoint that the central server calls to deliver user input (text, files) for Ralph to access. Writes received input to a known location on the VPS filesystem that ralph.py can read. (3) Watchdog: monitors ralph.py for unexpected crashes (non-graceful exits). If ralph.py crashes, controller.py notifies the central server, which can push a fixed ralph.py to the VPS. controller.py then applies the fix and restarts the loop. (4) Authentication uses a shared secret generated at provisioning time and stored in the DB. controller.py is managed by systemd (Restart=always, RestartSec=5, starts on boot). On startup, it checks local state to see if a Ralph loop was mid-run and resumes it automatically if so.
acceptance_criteria: controller.py runs on the VPS, forwards ralph.py stdout and events to the central server in real-time, receives user input and makes it available to Ralph, detects ralph.py crashes and applies fixes pushed from the central server, restarts automatically on crash via systemd, and resumes any interrupted Ralph loop on startup.
notes: If stuck: use a simple file-based queue or named pipe for local ralph.py ↔ controller.py communication. The central server's internal API endpoint and shared secret are injected at provisioning time via cloud-init.

title: Hetzner VPS provisioning API integration
description: When the user clicks 'Just Ralph It' for the first time on a project, the central server provisions a dedicated Hetzner VPS for that project via the Hetzner API. Steps: (1) create a new server from the pre-built snapshot, (2) assign a static IP (Hetzner floating IP), (3) pass project secrets via cloud-init (GitHub App installation token, Qwen or BYOK key, central server URL and shared secret), (4) wait for the server to be ready, (5) store the VPS IP, server ID, and static IP in the DB associated with the project. One VPS per project, provisioned on first loop start, destroyed on project deletion.
acceptance_criteria: A new user gets a dedicated VPS with a static IP provisioned automatically on signup. The VPS is reachable and has all tools configured. VPS details are stored in the DB.
notes: Security: all secrets (SECRET_KEY, shared auth token between VPS and central server) must be generated as cryptographically secure random values at provisioning time via cloud-init. Never use hardcoded defaults. The app must refuse to start if any required secret is missing. If stuck: use the Hetzner Cloud API (https://docs.hetzner.cloud). Use floating IPs for static addressing. Use cloud-init user_data for secret injection.

title: VPS snapshot with pre-installed tooling
description: Create a custom Linux VPS snapshot (base image) with all required binaries pre-installed: Python, Node.js, opencode, bd (beads), bdui, git, gh (GitHub CLI), Dolt. Also includes ralph.py, controller.py, and a setup.sh script that runs at first boot to inject secrets (API keys, GitHub credentials, central server URL and shared secret) passed as cloud-init user data. controller.py starts automatically on boot. The snapshot is used by the provisioning step to spin up new user VPSes quickly. Opencode config is god mode. No user-specific data is baked into the snapshot.
acceptance_criteria: A VPS spun up from the snapshot has all binaries available and setup.sh runs successfully on first boot, configuring the instance with injected secrets.
notes: If stuck: use Hetzner's snapshot API to create and store the image after manually configuring a base server. Use cloud-init for secret injection at boot time.

title: Per-project VPS provisioning
description: Provision a dedicated Hetzner VPS per project when the user clicks 'Just Ralph It' for the first time on that project. The VPS runs the Ralph loop and hosts any services Ralph deploys. It stays alive until the project is deleted. Billing: 1.5x Hetzner cost per project VPS per month, plus token usage.
acceptance_criteria: Clicking 'Just Ralph It' on a project provisions a dedicated VPS for that project. The VPS persists until the project is deleted. Multiple projects can have concurrent VPSes.

title: Stripe payments
description: Integrate Stripe to charge users for VPS provisioning and usage. Requires per-user VPS provisioning to exist first. Billing model TBD.
acceptance_criteria: Users are charged via Stripe for their VPS usage. Payment failures result in graceful degradation (e.g. loop paused, user notified).
